title: Follow These Rules for Successful Weight Loss

chunks.title: Introduction to Healthy Eating
chunks.summary: The discussion initiates with advice on maintaining a healthy weight long-term, emphasizing simplicity yet recognizing the challenges of achieving it. The speaker suggests focusing on single ingredient foods that are presumably recognizable to one's great-grandmother.
chunks.transcript: So, what would you tell people who are kind of feeling discouraged like what is the best way to maintain a healthy weight long term? >> I say it's simple, but it's not easy, right? So, it's simple. Single ingredient foods and foods that your great-grandmother would recognize if they have a label.

chunks.title: Processed Foods and Convenience
chunks.summary: The conversation shifts to the proliferation of processed foods designed for convenience, which can mislead consumers. The speaker highlights how processed items, even those claiming to be healthier, often contain problematic ingredients that the average consumer cannot recognize.
chunks.transcript: So, single ingredient foods, you walk in the grocery store, it's basically the outside of the grocery store. Eggs, milk, cheese, meat, chicken, fish, red meat, right? Cabbage, apple, cucumber, >> cabbage. I'm surprised. >> Well, I'm trying to be inclusive, right? If you want to eat if you want to eat salads, be my guest. But, you know, apple, orange, you know, pineapple, single ingredient of foods. You get the idea. >> If you're seeing a label, would my great-grandmother recognize the ingredients, right? Um, so most processed foods, my great-grandmother wouldn't recognize the ingredients, right? Like, so my great grandmother is reading the ingredients of a David bar. Uh, EPG. What is EPG? You know, and then the ingredients become artificial and natural flavors. I have no idea what that is. >> No one does. >> I'm channeling my great-grandmother now. And then you have, you know, sucralose, aspartame, AK, uh, like no one knows these ingredients and they're problematic for humans. So that's the simple rule. Like if you look at, for instance, a package. And I think the problem here, and the reason I say it's simple but not easy, is the convenience piece, >> right? So I can say eat single ingredient foods. It's easy, guys. And I know that your audience is going to say, "Okay, >> I work. I have children. It's hard. It's it's time." So, this is where the processed manufacturers get us is convenience. >> And this is what worries me about things like this David Bar or these protein forward Pop-Tarts or cereals is that I think processed food manufacturers are smart and they've seen the writing on their wall and they realize, "Hey, we're getting called out for Doritos and Coca-Cola. Let's make something that is one degree better." >> Wait, is that really a thing? Pop-Tarts with protein. Oh yeah. >> I haven't seen that one yet. >> Oh yeah. Protein Pop-Tarts. Yeah, absolutely. >> Oh my gosh. >> Protein brownies, protein cookies. Oh yeah. It's just like let's make something one degree better. Like this is the new iteration. And you see this with look, okay, anything is a step in the right direction. But it's like I think that they're trying to fool us in some ways and they're saying let's make things one degree better, one iteration

chunks.title: Struggles with Healthy Choices
chunks.summary: The importance of confronting the convenience of processed foods is emphasized. The speakers express concern that convenience often overshadows healthier choices, leading to poor dietary decisions and the difficulty in returning to simpler, whole foods.
chunks.transcript: better. And instead of having a Snickers bar, you have a bar with um whey protein and collagen, but it also has EPG. you know, this fake fat, it's going to cause loss of fats, soluble vitamins, and it has artificial flavors. >> Yeah, it's like the it's these labeling games. I mean, the same thing happens in the skincare world. Like, consumers know what to put on the label to make people feel just a little bit better about what they're using on their skin. And the same thing happens in food, >> just a little bit better, right? And so, at this rate, we're never going to get back to good food, right? So, you have to take a quantum leap and say, "Okay, I'm going to just start eating good food." like this the number of steps between like okay you have Snickers bar maybe the next step is a David bar but it's like 25 steps to get back to a freaking chicken breast right whereas you could get the same amount of protein in a David bar with the same amount of calories in a chicken breast and way more micron like you have a it's the Grand Canyon divide there we're just we're never going to get there at this rate so but people get confused and and they leverage convenience so they make something that's one degree better and convenient and so that's why I mean that's the whole reason I built Lineage you know we built Lineage is Like I wanted to try as best I could in my little small corner of the world to make something that's convenient but high quality because like you have to fight back somehow. And look, I would much rather that someone just make a ribeye steak, but if you're running around like >> when I looked at the market like jerkys and meat stick, there's nothing that I really liked. And so like let me just make that for people. And that that's sort of my answer. It's like let me create this for you because I'm in this unique situation that I never thought I'd be in and I want to help you with the convenience piece. The fact of the matter is that if we let convenience dictate too many of our decisions, it's just so hard to make good decisions. I flew here yesterday from Florida and you look in the airport and I do this now. It's like my thing. I just look at all the foods that you can get. I'm thinking like what is even here? >> Yeah. >> What is the health this if we let convenience be the arbiter of the choices we make with food? It's very hard. >> The convenience is killing us

chunks.title: Intentional Eating and Satiety
chunks.summary: The speakers discuss the significance of intentional choices in diet and how it affects overall well-being. They argue that establishing good, simple eating habits can enhance energy levels and improve many aspects of life, including hormonal balance and sleep.
chunks.transcript: essentially. And I know that you mentioned like it's simple but it's not easy. chunks.title: Concerns about Artificial Sweeteners
chunks.summary: A study regarding artificial sweeteners suggests negative health impacts, including an influence on hunger signals. The speakers argue that despite claims of safety, these sweeteners may contribute to increased hunger and undermine satiety, contrasting with whole food options.
chunks.transcript: making these intentional choices. >> It's a total it's just a mindset shift. >> Yeah. >> But that's a hard thing for people. It's meaningful and it's lasting, but it's challenging. And I love that you brought up the satiety piece because I want to just mention this for a minute because I think this is sinister. There's a study that just came out a few months ago. So, we talked about EPG, you know, horrible artificial flavors, natural flavors, not great for humans, but these artificial sweeteners, I think, are sinister. And I've talked about these for years now. And >> what are some examples of the art? >> Superlo, ACK, and aspartame are the big ones. And ace K is sometimes as fame K on the label. Sucralose is sometimes as Splenda is the brand name. So those are the three big ones. And I've been talking about these for years. And whenever I would talk about it, people in the health space would say, "Look, Paul, here's a huge metaanalysis that shows drinking diet soda is weight neutral compared to drinking or even better than drinking soda with sugar." Right? So you're So there's nothing to worry about with these artificial sweeteners. And I think like yeah, you're missing the forest for the trees. like they definitely affect the way the microbiome um is communicating. So there's quorum sensor sensing is a word that we used to talk about how bacteria and viruses and fungi and our guts communicate with each other. The artificial sweeteners definitely change that potentially in a negative way. And a recent study came out with sucralose showing that it increases blood flow to the hypothalamus. So it's it's changing the way your brain reacts to food in a way that it's probably making you hungrier. So, here is at least the mechanistic evidence to say this is not benign. Like, you can show show me this metaanalysis, but I don't buy it. I just don't buy it. And I just don't think that any human that has intuition is going to look at a protein bar or a protein Pop-Tart because all these protein Pop-Tarts and protein cookies have these artificial sweeteners because they're marketed as zero sugar, which is great because processed sugar is not good for humans, but humans still want it to taste sweet because we have this evolutionary desire for carbohydrates from our history with sweet fruit, which is a great thing. >> And so you put these artificial sweeteners in. Look guys, it's fine. and it's safe, no problem. But then you see studies like this and it's like, wait, it's actually probably sabotaging your society.
